title: Gödel's Proof
subtitle: An Accessible Explanation of Kurt Gödel's Incompleteness Theorems
author: Ernest Nagel, James R. Newman
themes: The problem of consistency in formal axiomatic systems
The distinction between mathematics and meta-mathematics
The limits of formal proof and decidability
The relationship between truth and provability
awards: One of the most widely-read explanations of Gödel's theorems
Revised edition with introduction by Douglas R. Hofstadter (2001)
Chosen as a Scientific American recommended book
Featured in The Guardian: 'They accomplish the wondrous task of clarifying the argumentative outline of Kurt Gödel's celebrated logic bomb.'
excerpt: The classic layperson's guide to Gödel's incompleteness theorems. Nagel and Newman demystify one of the most celebrated and misunderstood results of 20th-century mathematics — Gödel's proof that any sufficiently powerful formal system is either incomplete or inconsistent — using clear historical exposition and a step-by-step walkthrough of Gödel's breathtaking self-referential construction.
